Name: spectral_stability_pruning
Title: Spectral Stability-Aware Computation Pruning for Latency-Bounded Inference
Short Hypothesis: Tokens with stable spectral properties can be processed with fewer computations without compromising accuracy, enabling latency-aware inference.
Related Work: Existing work focuses on precision allocation (Spectral Quantization Routing), interference cancellation (Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation), and subspace filtering (Geometric Signal Separation). This proposal uniquely targets computation pruning based on spectral stability, filling a gap in the literature.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Stability-Aware Computation Pruning (SSACP), a training-free framework that dynamically prunes computations during inference based on the spectral stability of hidden states. By computing the condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states on-the-fly, SSACP identifies tokens that can be safely processed with fewer computations (e.g., skipping layers or pruning attention heads) without compromising accuracy. We theoretically ground this approach using PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral stability to generalization under computation pruning. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SSACP achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to baseline models. By replacing heuristic pruning strategies with formalized spectral stability diagnostics, SSACP provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under strict latency budgets.
Experiments: Evaluate SSACP on WILDS benchmarks under natural distribution shifts, measuring accuracy retention and latency reduction.
Compare SSACP to baseline models and heuristic pruning strategies, assessing throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Analyze the correlation between spectral stability metrics (condition number, spectral decay rate) and pruning success rates.
Conduct ablation studies to validate the impact of spectral stability diagnostics on pruning policies.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Spectral stability metrics may not generalize to all tasks or architectures.
On-the-fly computation of spectral properties may introduce overhead, though we mitigate this with randomized SVD.
The approach assumes that spectral stability is a reliable proxy for computation redundancy, which may not hold in all cases.
